Note: many details will change later, but the thinking pattern remains.

title: Stage 2 – Stakeholder Needs & Requirements
section: ECE Example – Wearable Medical Monitor (Stakeholder Requirements)
text:
ECE Example – Wearable Medical Monitor (Stakeholder Requirements)


Context: Wearable device that monitors heart rate and activity for post‑surgery patients.
Stakeholders: - Patients - Nurses - Cardiologists - Hospital IT - Regulatory / ethics board
Sample STR-*:

STR-SY-201: Patients shall be able to wear the device comfortably for at least 16 hours per day during the monitoring period.
STR-SY-202: Nurses shall receive an alert within 5 minutes when heart rate exceeds a configured threshold for more than 2 minutes.


More STR-*:

STR-SY-203: Cardiologists shall be able to review summarized daily reports of heart rate and activity trends for each patient.
STR-SY-204: The system shall store and transmit patient data in compliance with hospital privacy policies (e.g., encryption in transit and at rest as required).
STR-SY-205: Hospital IT staff shall be able to manage user accounts and access using the existing hospital authentication system.

Planned validation ideas:

Patient usability trial (comfort, wearing time).
Clinical staff demo using anonymized data.
Security / compliance review with IT and ethics board.



Use this to show: - Stakeholder requirements can include comfort, privacy, integration with existing systems – not just “measure heart rate accurately.” - Many STR-* will later lead to quite complex technical REQ-* and tests, but the stakeholder framing stays simple and clear.

title: Stage 2 – Stakeholder Needs & Requirements
text:
Common Pitfalls in Stage 2

Vague requirements

“System should be fast,” “should be user‑friendly,” “should be secure” with no further detail.

Hidden assumptions

Assuming lab techs have dual monitors or special permissions.
Assuming students always have latest smartphones.

Missing stakeholders

Ignoring IT, maintenance staff, or safety officers.

Jumping to design

Writing STR-* that dictate internal technologies:

“System shall use Bluetooth X,” “shall use Raspberry Pi 4,” etc.









Warning


Stage 2 is about what stakeholders need, not about picking your favorite chips or frameworks.




Encourage students to use Stage 2 reviews to catch and fix these pitfalls before they become baked into the project.
You can show one or two “bad” example requirements and ask them how to improve.

title: Stage 2 – Stakeholder Needs & Requirements
text:
Summary – Stage 2 Key Takeaways

Stage 2 – Stakeholder Needs & Requirements uses 29148’s:

Stakeholder Needs & Requirements Definition
Early Requirements Validation (planning)

Your main job is to:

Elicit and document stakeholder needs as clear STR-* requirements.
Organize them into a Stakeholder Requirements Specification (StRS) (DCP‑100B).
Plan how you will later validate these needs (VAL-* planning).

Good STR-* are:

Understandable to stakeholders, non‑ambiguous, realistic, and validation‑oriented.

DCP‑100B is the Stage 2 gate artifact:

Without solid STR-* and initial validation planning, Stage 3 (System Requirements) will be built on shaky ground.








Important


Stage 2 is where you lock in what success means for your stakeholders. Everything else in the project exists to satisfy and prove those STR-*.




Close by: - Reiterating the connection between STR-* in Stage 2 and VAL-* in Stage 8. - Assigning work: draft at least 10 candidate STR-* derived from your DCP‑100A, and map each to a stakeholder and scenario.
Invite targeted questions: - How many STR-* are typical? - How precise should they be at this point? - How to handle conflicting stakeholder needs?

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Capstone Design Course Introduction
From Needs to Validated Systems using the V‑Model & ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148
Document Context: DCP‑000 Course Guide, Rev 1.0

This deck is the first session introduction for the Capstone Design course. Goal: give students a big‑picture roadmap of the course, explain why we use the V‑Model and 29148, and how the DCP document series and traceability tie everything together. Remind them: this is not “just a project course”; it is a systems engineering lab that simulates industry practice.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Session Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain the overall V‑Model lifecycle used in the course.
Describe the nine stages of the Capstone project and their main deliverables.
Explain the difference between verification and validation.
Use the basic ID and traceability scheme (STR, REQ, CMP, IMP, TST, VER, VAL).
Understand the DCP document package and gate reviews.


Emphasize that this lecture is meta‑level: today is about how the course works, not technical content. Students should leave understanding what success looks like administratively and methodologically.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Why a Structured Capstone?


Engineering reality:

Real systems are complex (HW + SW + users + environment).
Failing late is expensive (money, safety, reputation).
Industry uses process models (V‑Model, 29148, 15288) to reduce risk.

Course philosophy:

Your capstone is a mini‑industry project, not just a demo.
You will practice the full lifecycle from need → requirements → design → code/hardware → tests → handover.
We grade both technical quality and engineering discipline.


Real‑world ECE examples:

Medical device firmware must trace every requirement to a test (FDA).
Automotive ECUs follow V‑Model (ISO 26262) to prove safety.
Telecom base stations use detailed requirements / architecture for complex RF + DSP chains.








Important


Key message: You’re learning how real engineering organizations work.




Use real anecdotes if you have them (e.g., a product recall from a requirement misunderstanding). Point out that many students will interview at companies that ask about requirements, testing, traceability, not just coding syntax or basic circuits.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Standards Backbone for the Course


Core standards guiding this course:

ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering
ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288:2023 System life cycle processes — provides the V‑Model context.

We map the course to these by:

Structuring stages according to 29148 processes.
Using the V‑Model as the project roadmap.
Emphasizing requirements quality, verification, validation, and traceability.















Clarify that students are not expected to memorize the standard numbers, but they should understand the concepts those standards enforce: - Good requirements. - Systematic verification/validation. - Lifecycle thinking.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Core Principles of the Capstone

Requirements before solutions

Understand the problem and needs before jumping to circuits, code, or schematics.

Traceability always

Every artifact (requirement, design element, test) is linked up and down.
The Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) is your single source of truth.

Verifiable & Validated

Verification: “Did we build it right?”
Validation: “Did we build the right thing?”








Tip


Think of requirements like a contract: You can’t claim you met the contract unless each clause is testable and tested.




This slide defines the mental model for the whole course. Use a simple story (e.g., building a smart thermostat): - Requirement: “User can set temperature between 16–30°C with ±0.5°C accuracy.” - Verification: bench‑test the temp sensor and control algorithm. - Validation: check if actual users are comfortable and find the UI usable.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
The V‑Model – Big Picture

















Note


Left side: Define & design.
Right side: Test & prove.
Bottom: Implementation.




Point out the mirror relationships:

Stakeholder needs ↔︎ Stakeholder validation.
System requirements ↔︎ System verification.
Component design ↔︎ Unit tests.

Students will live this structure through the nine stages.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Course Stages Overview
We implement the V‑Model in nine stages:

Project Framing & Concept of Operations (ConOps)
Stakeholder Requirements
System Requirements
Architecture & Component Design
Implementation
Unit / Component Verification
System / Integration Verification
Stakeholder Validation
Project Finalization & Handover

Each stage:

Has a clear purpose.
Produces a DCP document.
Is gated by a review before moving on.


Tell students: “Think of this like levels in a game. You can’t move to Level 3 until you beat the boss at Level 2 (gate review).” This helps them anticipate workload and milestones across the year.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Traceability: The Backbone of the Project
text:
Traceability: The Backbone of the Project


Artifacts and IDs:

Stakeholder Requirement → STR-SY-### or STR-SW-###
System Requirement → REQ-SY-### or REQ-SW-###
Component → CMP-SY-### or CMP-SW-###
Implementation Artifact → IMP-SY-### or IMP-SW-###
Unit Test → TST-SY-### or TST-SW-###
System Verification Test → VER-SY-### or VER-SW-###
Validation Activity → VAL-SY-### or VAL-SW-###

All of these live in the Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM).

Traceability Links:













Explain vertical traceability: STR → REQ → CMP → IMP → TST → VER → VAL. Mention horizontal traceability: - Stage 2 ↔︎ Stage 8 (STR ↔︎ VAL) - Stage 3 ↔︎ Stage 7 (REQ ↔︎ VER) - Stage 4 ↔︎ Stage 6 (CMP ↔︎ TST)
Students will maintain this in a spreadsheet or requirements tool.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Traceability in the V‑Model

















Note


Horizontal links:

STR-* ↔︎ VAL-* (Stakeholder needs ↔︎ Acceptance tests)
REQ-* ↔︎ VER-* (System requirements ↔︎ System tests)
CMP-* ↔︎ TST-* (Component design ↔︎ Unit tests)





This is the “V‑Model as a graph” where nodes are artifacts and edges are traceability links. Use an example: - STR-SY-001: “Lab tech can measure temperature remotely.” - REQ-SY-003: “System shall transmit temperature updates every 1 s with ±0.5°C accuracy.” - CMP-SY-010: “Wireless module & protocol design.” - TST-SY-007: “Unit test for wireless throughput and reliability.” - VER-SY-002: “System‑level test of telemetry performance.” - VAL-SY-001: “User acceptance test in actual lab environment.”

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations


V‑Model Level: Top‑left – Concept / Need
29148 Processes:

Business or Mission Analysis
Stakeholder Identification

Capstone Activities:

Define the problem domain and business/mission need.
Analyze existing solutions and their limitations.
Characterize environment: users, constraints, stakeholders.
Select a solution class (not a detailed design).
Produce a Concept of Operations (ConOps).


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑100A Project Proposal

Problem statement
Stakeholder map
Operational scenarios
High‑level success criteria








Tip


Focus on the “why”, not on schematics or specific chips yet.





Warn students against “premature design fixation”.
If someone says, “We’re building with Raspberry Pi and LoRa,” ask:

What problem are you solving?
Who are your stakeholders?
What are the operational scenarios?

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 2 – Stakeholder Needs & Requirements


V‑Model Level: Upper‑left – Stakeholder / User Requirements
29148 Processes:

Stakeholder Needs & Requirements Definition
Early Requirements Validation (planning)

Capstone Activities:

Identify all stakeholders: advisors, clients, end‑users, regulators, lab staff.
Conduct interviews, surveys, observations.
Use use cases, user stories, and scenarios to elicit needs.
Prioritize needs (e.g., MoSCoW, Kano).
Resolve conflicts and negotiate scope.
Develop a Stakeholder Requirements Specification (StRS).


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑100B Stakeholder Requirements

STR-* IDs
High‑level acceptance criteria
Initial Validation Plan (VAL-* planned)


Quality Criteria (29148):

Necessary
Unambiguous
Complete (to agreed scope)
Traceable to business needs



Use a quick in‑class exercise: Give them a vague requirement like “System should be easy to use” and ask how to make it measurable and testable (e.g., “User shall complete basic task X within 2 minutes without external help”).

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements


V‑Model Level: Mid‑left – System Requirements
29148 Processes:

System/Software Requirements Definition
Requirements Analysis

Capstone Activities:

Transform stakeholder needs (StRS) into technical requirements.
Categorize:

Functional
Performance
Interface
Security
Usability
Environmental / Reliability / Safety

Define acceptance criteria and verification methods:

Test, Analysis, Model, Demonstration, Inspection (TAMDI).

Ensure requirements are:

Verifiable, Feasible, Singular (one requirement per statement).

Produce SyRS/SRS.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑200 System Requirements

REQ-* IDs
Bidirectional traceability: STR-* → REQ-*
Initial Verification Plan (VER-* planned)


Example requirement form:
\[
\text{REQ-SY-023: The system shall}~ <\text{observable behavior}> \\
\text{under}~ <\text{conditions}> ~\text{with}~ <\text{quantitative criteria}>.
\]


Clarify the difference between StRS and SyRS/SRS:

StRS: stakeholder language (“The lab wants to …”).
SyRS: engineering language, quantitative, testable.

Use a simple example:

STR: “Student should be able to log in quickly.”
REQ: “System shall authenticate a user within 2 s in 95% of attempts over campus Wi‑Fi.”

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 4 – Architecture & Component Design


V‑Model Level: Mid‑left – Architecture and Detailed Design
29148 / 15288 Role:

Architecture Definition
Design Definition
Requirements Allocation

Capstone Activities:

Develop system architecture:

Block diagrams
Data flow, control flow, or state diagrams

Allocate each REQ-* to one or more components (CMP-*).
Define interfaces (signals, protocols, voltages, APIs).
Develop detailed design:

Algorithms, data structures (SW)
Schematics, PCB layouts (HW)

Update RTM: REQ-* → CMP-*.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑300 Design

Architecture views
Component specifications (CMP-*)
Requirements Allocation Matrix
Initial Test Plan (TST-* planned)




















Important


Every component should exist because it satisfies at least one requirement. If you can’t map a component to a requirement, question why it exists.




This is where many projects either stay coherent or become a “bag of parts.” Highlight interface definitions as common failure points in integration.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 5 – Implementation / Construction


V‑Model Level: Bottom – Coding / Building
29148 / 15288 Role:

Implementation Process
Configuration Management

Capstone Activities:

Implement the design:

Develop software modules (embedded, apps, services).
Build hardware (prototypes, PCBs, mechanical assemblies).

Use version control (Git) and branching strategies.
Perform code / design reviews against requirements.
Manage change requests when implementation forces requirement/design updates.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑400 Implementation

Implementation artifacts (IMP-*)
Build procedures
Updated requirements baseline & change log








Note


Expect iterative build–test–fix cycles.
The key is to manage changes and keep traceability up to date.





Make clear that this is the most “hands‑on” stage, but good work here depends on good requirements and design upstream.
Use a quick example of a Git log entry referencing a requirement ID (REQ-SW-012) in the commit message as a traceability practice.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 6 – Unit / Component Verification


V‑Model Level: Lower‑right – Unit Testing / Component Verification
29148 Process: Verification at component level
Capstone Activities:

Design and execute unit tests for each component (CMP-*).
Map each test (TST-*) to:

Component(s)
Allocated requirements (REQ-*)

Record test procedures, environments, and results.
Log defects and perform root‑cause analysis.
Update RTM: CMP-* → TST-*.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑500A Unit Verification

TST-* test cases
Unit test logs
Defect reports








Tip


For every test case, be able to answer:

“Which requirement does this verify?”






For ECE:

Unit test for a DSP filter module: check frequency response vs spec.
Unit test for a sensor board: measure offset, noise, linearity vs REQ-SY-0xx. You can show simple pseudo‑code or a testbench listing referencing requirement IDs in comments.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 7 – System / Integration Verification


V‑Model Level: Mid‑right – Integration & System Test
29148 Process: Verification against system requirements
Capstone Activities:

Integrate components incrementally (e.g., bottom‑up, top‑down, or mixed).
Test interfaces and interactions (timing, protocols, data consistency).
Execute system test procedures derived from SyRS/SRS.
Ensure full coverage of REQ-* in system verification.
Update RTM: REQ-* ↔︎ VER-*.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑500B System Verification

VER-* system test cases and results
Integration & system test reports
Updated RTM with coverage metrics








Important


Verification = “Did we build it right?” Does the integrated system meet all system requirements?





Highlight emergent behavior: problems that only appear when subsystems interact. Examples in ECE: RF interference when two subsystems are close, timing issues between microcontroller and peripheral, race conditions in distributed systems, etc.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 8 – Stakeholder Validation & Acceptance


V‑Model Level: Top‑right – System / Acceptance Testing
29148 Process: Validation
Capstone Activities:

Execute system‑level tests against stakeholder needs (StRS).
Conduct acceptance testing with real stakeholders.
Observe users in realistic environments or scenarios.
Ask:


“Does this system solve the original business/mission problem?”


Gather feedback, capture issues or change requests.
Produce Validation Report and Acceptance Sign‑off.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑500C Stakeholder Validation

VAL-* validation activities and results
Stakeholder demo summary
Lessons learned from usage








Important


Validation = “Did we build the right thing?” Even a perfectly verified system can fail validation if it doesn’t meet real needs.





Use a simple story: A system that meets every technical spec but has a terrible UI and users refuse to adopt it → verification succeeded, validation failed. Encourage teams to involve stakeholders early and often (e.g., quick UI mockup sessions).

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 9 – Project Finalization & Handover


V‑Model Level: Spans entire V – Transition & Closure
29148 / 15288 Processes:

Information Management
Transition
Disposal Planning (at least conceptually)

Capstone Activities:

Finalize all documentation:

StRS, SyRS/SRS
Design docs
Test reports
User & maintenance manuals

Freeze and archive the Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM).
Conduct a project post‑mortem (lessons learned).
Present final system to faculty, sponsors, and stakeholders.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑600 Final Report & Handover

Complete RTM
Project archive (source, CAD, configs)
Handover instructions








Note


Think like a professional engineer handing a system to a client or another engineering team.





Ask: “If you left the company tomorrow, could someone else maintain or extend your system using only your final package?” Use this to motivate clean documentation, code comments, and design rationale.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Teaching Topics by Stage (Roadmap)







Stage
Focus Topics




1
Problem scoping, stakeholder analysis, ConOps, problem vs solution space, systems thinking


2
Elicitation techniques, characteristics of good StRS, use cases, MoSCoW, ambiguity review


3
Functional vs non‑functional, 29148 requirement characteristics, TAMDI verification methods, constraints


4
Architecture views, requirement allocation, interfaces, make/buy, design rationale


5
Configuration management, baselines, change control, technical debt, integration planning


6
Unit test design, code coverage, requirement‑based testing, defect tracking


7
Integration strategy, system test design, emergent properties, test environments


8
Validation planning, usability testing, stakeholder feedback, “fit for purpose”


9
Documentation, handover, maintenance planning, post‑mortem, ethics




Use this slide to set expectations for upcoming lectures / labs. Students can see how theoretical topics (e.g., requirements quality) map to deliverables (e.g., DCP‑200).

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Example: Smart Lab Sensor Network (ECE Context)
text:
Example: Smart Lab Sensor Network (ECE Context)


Concept:
Design a wireless sensor network that monitors temperature and humidity in teaching labs and pushes data to a dashboard.
Sample Stakeholder Requirements (STR-*):

STR-SY-001: Lab technicians shall monitor each lab’s temperature in real time from a central dashboard.
STR-SY-002: System shall alert staff when temperature in any lab exceeds 28°C for more than 10 minutes.


Derived System Requirements (REQ-*):

REQ-SY-010: Each sensor node shall measure temperature from 10–40°C with ±0.5°C accuracy.
REQ-SY-011: Each node shall transmit measurements at least once every 60 s.
REQ-SW-020: The server software shall generate an alert within 2 min of detecting a lab exceeding 28°C for 10 min.

Potential Tests (TST-*, VER-*, VAL-*):

TST-SY-001: Bench‑test temperature sensor calibration.
VER-SW-002: System‑level test of data latency and alert generation.
VAL-SY-001: Validation session with lab staff to confirm alerts are useful and understandable.



Walk through this example across the stages:

Stage 1: Why monitor labs? Who cares?
Stage 2: What do technicians, facilities, and students need?
Stage 3: Convert those into quantitative specs.
Stage 4: Architecture (nodes, gateways, server, dashboard).
Stage 5: HW + SW implementation.
Stage 6–7: Testing sensors, network, alerts.
Stage 8: Let real lab staff try the system and collect feedback.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Summary – What You Should Take Away Today
text:
Summary – What You Should Take Away Today

The course uses the V‑Model and ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 to structure your capstone.
You will work through nine stages from problem framing to handover.
Traceability is central: STR-* → REQ-* → CMP-* → IMP-* → TST-* → VER-* → VAL-*.
DCP documents (DCP‑100A … DCP‑600) are your official artifacts for gate reviews.
Verification asks: “Did we build it right?” Validation asks: “Did we build the right thing?”







Important


Your success is not just the final demo. It is demonstrating that you can engineer a system professionally, from needs to validated solution.




Close by connecting this to students’ future careers and accreditation outcomes (ABET, etc., if applicable). Invite questions specifically about: - Scope of projects - Team composition - Tools for RTM (spreadsheets, requirements plugins, etc.)

title: About
text: Capstone Design Course Notes.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
From Needs to Validated Systems using the V‑Model & ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148
Capstone Design – Stage 1 Focus Session

This is the first deep‑dive session into Stage 1 of the capstone process. Students have already seen the big picture (V‑Model, nine stages).
Goal of this deck: - Zoom in on Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations. - Explain 29148: Business or Mission Analysis and Stakeholder Identification in concrete, undergraduate‑friendly terms. - Show how this becomes DCP‑100A Project Proposal. - Use ECE‑relevant examples so they see why this matters for their projects.
Remind them: Stage 1 is about what & why, not how. No schematics, no detailed code yet – those come later.




By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain the purpose of Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations (ConOps).
Describe the 29148 Business or Mission Analysis process and its typical activities.
Describe the 29148 Stakeholder Identification process and why it is critical.
List the key contents and deliverables of DCP‑100A Project Proposal.
Apply these ideas to ECE capstone examples and avoid “solution first” thinking.


Emphasize: these are practical objectives. Students should walk away knowing exactly what they must produce for Stage 1 and why each part exists in industry practice.










{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  subgraph Left["Definition & Design"]
    A["Business / Mission<br>Analysis (Stage 1)"]
    B["Stakeholder Needs<br>& Requirements (Stage 2)"]
    C["System / SW Requirements<br>(Stage 3)"]
    D["Architecture & Component<br>Design (Stage 4)"]
    E["Implementation<br>(Stage 5)"]
  end

  subgraph Right["Integration & Qualification"]
    F["Unit / Component<br>Verification (Stage 6)"]
    G["System / Integration<br>Verification (Stage 7)"]
    H["Stakeholder Validation<br>& Acceptance (Stage 8)"]
  end

  A --> B --> C --> D --> E
  E --> F --> G --> H

  style A fill:#ffd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px












Note



Stage 1 lives at the very top‑left: - Define the business/mission need. - Understand the operational context. - Identify who cares (stakeholders).



Draw their attention to the highlighted Stage 1 box. Explain: if Stage 1 is weak or rushed, everything downstream (requirements, design, tests) will be misaligned.
Analogy: if you start building a bridge without agreeing on where it needs to connect and what it should carry (cars? trains?), the detailed design can be “beautiful” but completely wrong.







In course terms (from the intro deck):

V‑Model Level: Concept / Need
29148 Processes:

Business or Mission Analysis
Stakeholder Identification


Capstone Activities (Stage 1):

Define the problem domain and business/mission need.
Analyze existing solutions and their limitations.
Characterize environment & context:

Users
Constraints
External systems

Select a solution class (type of solution), not a design.
Produce a Concept of Operations (ConOps).


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑100A – Project Proposal

Problem statement
Stakeholder map
Operational scenarios
High‑level success criteria








Tip



Stage 1 is about the story of the project: Who has what problem, in what context, and what would “success” look like?





Connect back to the earlier “Course Stages Overview” slide. Tell them: DCP‑100A is the artifact that proves you did Stage 1 properly. Gate review for Stage 1 will focus strongly on: - Clarity of problem. - Completeness of stakeholder picture. - Realistic, well‑explained ConOps.







In Stage 1 we primarily apply:

Business or Mission Analysis

Why does this system exist?
What business or mission outcomes must it support?

Stakeholder Identification

Who cares about this system?
Who is affected, who operates it, who maintains it, who pays for it?


Later stages use other 29148 processes (Stakeholder Needs, System Requirements, etc.), but Stage 1 is the front door.






{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Business / Mission<br>Analysis"]
  B["Stakeholder<br>Identification"]
  C["Stakeholder Needs<br>& Requirements"]
  D["System Requirements"]
  A --> B --> C --> D

  style A fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
  style B fill:#ffd,stroke:#333









Clarify that 29148 is a requirements engineering standard. We’re using only a subset of its processes.
For this lecture, we’ll zoom into: - What Business/Mission Analysis looks like in a capstone context. - How to systematically identify stakeholders so nobody critical is forgotten.





Purpose: To understand the business context or mission context that drives the need for a new or modified system.
Typical questions it answers:

What problem or opportunity are we addressing?
What is the mission (for non‑commercial systems) or business goal (for commercial ones)?
What outcomes do stakeholders care about?
What is the operational environment (physical, technical, organizational)?
What constraints limit possible solutions?







Important



If you can’t articulate the mission/business case, you don’t yet have a good project.



Use examples that students understand: - Mission: “Provide safe, reliable power to a remote village.” - Business goal: “Reduce lab equipment downtime by 30% to save maintenance costs.”
Explain: Engineers often get “assignments” like “build an app” or “design a sensor node,” but 29148 wants us to ask why first.







Business‑oriented example:
Project: Smart Lab Energy Monitor

Business problem: Lab electricity bills are high; facilities wants to cut costs without compromising experiments.
Desired outcomes:

Identify energy‑hungry equipment and usage patterns.
Provide actionable data to lab managers.



Mission‑oriented example:
Project: Disaster‑Resilient Sensor Network

Mission context: Emergency services need situational awareness after earthquakes or floods.
Desired outcomes:

Maintain communication when mains power and some infrastructure are down.
Provide key sensor data (e.g., water level, gas leak detection) to responders.





Use these to highlight the difference: - Business: focus on cost, efficiency, competitive advantage. - Mission: focus on public safety, research goals, teaching, etc.
But both require clear problem framing and outcomes before we talk about specific hardware or software.





In 29148, activities commonly include:

Problem / Opportunity Definition

Describe current situation (AS‑IS).
Describe desired future situation (TO‑BE).

Context and Environment Analysis

Operational environment (physical, network, regulatory).
Systems and processes that interact with your proposed system.

Operations Concept Analysis

High‑level description of how the system will be used over time.

Feasibility Consideration

Technical feasibility (can we build it?).
Resource feasibility (time, budget, skills).

Business/Mission Case Formulation

Rationale for doing the project (benefits vs costs/risks).



Translate “standard‑speak” into capstone‑speak:

You’re not writing a full corporate business case, but you are expected to:

Clearly state the problem.
Analyze the context.
Outline how the proposed system will be used (ConOps).
Argue that the project is worth doing and feasible in two semesters.












{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Business / Mission Analysis<br>(Stage 1)"]
  B["Stakeholder Needs &<br>Requirements (Stage 2)"]
  C["System Requirements<br>(Stage 3)"]

  A --> B --> C

  classDef hl fill:#ffd,stroke:#333;
  class A hl;







Stage 1:

What is the problem or mission?
In what context do we operate?
What high‑level outcomes do we want?

Stage 2 & 3:

Turn this context into requirements.
First in stakeholder language (Stage 2), then in technical form (Stage 3).








Note



Stage 1 sets the direction; Stages 2–3 formalize what exactly must the system do.



Sometimes students try to write detailed requirements in Stage 1. Clarify: Stage 1 should stop at “what problem, what context, what kind of solution”. Precision and testability come later.





Purpose: To identify all parties that have an interest in the system or are affected by it, so their needs can be captured later.
“Stakeholder” includes:

Direct end users
Operators and maintainers
Managers and decision‑makers
Safety or regulatory bodies
IT/network admins, lab technicians
Faculty advisors, sponsors, teaching staff (in our course)







Warning



Missing a critical stakeholder early → hidden requirements pop up late, causing rework, scope creep, or project failure.



Example: A team builds a lab monitoring system only thinking of professors as stakeholders. Later they discover: - Lab technicians need maintenance alerts. - IT department cares about network security. - Facilities team needs specific data formats.
Each “late discovery” means rework. Stakeholder identification aims to minimize that.







Typical stakeholder roles in our projects:

Primary users

Students, lab technicians, clinicians, drivers, etc.

System owners / sponsors

Faculty PI, industry partner, lab manager.

Support and maintenance

IT support, lab techs, biomedical engineers.

Regulators / compliance

Safety office, ethics board, sometimes external standards (e.g., FCC, medical).



In this course specifically, also include:

Course staff

Instructor, TA → care about grading, documentation, safety.

University services

Campus network/security, facilities.

Future teams

Students who may extend or maintain your system next year.







{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  P["Proposed System"]
  U["Users"]
  O["Owners / Sponsors"]
  S["Support / Maintainers"]
  R["Regulators / Policies"]
  C["Course Staff"]

  U --> P
  O --> P
  S --> P
  R --> P
  C --> P









Stress that “stakeholder” is broader than “the person who asked for the project.” Encourage teams to be inclusive when brainstorming stakeholders, then prioritize which ones are most critical for Stage 1.





As described in 29148, tailored to our course:

Identify candidate stakeholders

Brainstorm a list of all who might interact with or be affected by the system.

Classify / group stakeholders

Users, owners, maintainers, regulators, external systems, etc.

Analyze stakeholder interests

What does each group want from the system?
What concerns or constraints do they have?

Prioritize stakeholders

Which ones are critical to success or acceptance?

Document a stakeholder map

For DCP‑100A: roles, interests, potential conflicts.








Tip



Treat stakeholder identification as a living activity – you may discover new stakeholders later, but Stage 1 should capture the obvious major ones.



Make this tangible: - Suggest using a simple table or diagram listing each stakeholder group and their key interests. - Mention that this feeds directly into Stage 2 (Stakeholder Requirements), where we turn these interests into STR-*.







29148 Says You Should:

Analyze business/mission context.
Identify and classify stakeholders.
Understand the operational environment.

In this course, you will:

Write a Problem Statement and Context section.
Create a Stakeholder Map.
Define Operational Scenarios / Use cases.
Propose a high‑level concept of operations (ConOps).


All of this goes into:


Sections typically include:

Background & Problem Description
Business/Mission Need and Objectives
Stakeholder Identification & Roles
Operational Context & Environment
Concept of Operations (ConOps)
Preliminary Solution Class and Scope
Risks & Feasibility (high level)





Remind students: DCP‑100A does not lock every detail forever. But it does become a baseline for later traceability. If the mission or stakeholders change significantly later, that’s a controlled change, not a casual pivot.





A Concept of Operations (ConOps) is a narrative and graphical description of:

How the system will be used in its real environment.
Who interacts with it and what they do.
Sequences of events (before, during, after use).
Operational modes (normal operation, startup, shutdown, failure modes, maintenance).

It is written mainly in stakeholder language, not low‑level engineering details.






Note



Think of ConOps as a storyboard of your system in action.



Contrast ConOps with design docs: - ConOps: “Lab technician logs in, sees alerts, acknowledges them.” - Design: “We use MQTT broker X, JSON payload Y, database Z.”
In Stage 1 we focus on ConOps; specific design choices belong in later stages.





A good ConOps in DCP‑100A should cover:

Overview of the system’s purpose

One or two paragraphs in plain language.

Operational environment

Physical: lab, vehicle, hospital room, outdoor, etc.
Technical: network, power, existing systems.

Operational scenarios

Step‑by‑step story of typical use cases.
Include normal and abnormal (error/failure) cases.

Roles and responsibilities

Who does what during operation, setup, maintenance.

Assumptions and constraints

“We assume campus Wi‑Fi is available.”
“System must run on battery for at least 8 hours.”



Tell students: ConOps is where you clearly show reviewers that you understand real‑world use, not just technology.
Gate reviewers will ask: “Is this scenario realistic? Are key actors included? Are constraints recognized?”







Example Project: Smart Lab Sensor Network (from intro deck).
Scenario 1 – Normal Operation:

Sensors in each lab periodically measure temperature and humidity.
Nodes send data wirelessly to a gateway.
Gateway forwards data to a campus server.
Dashboard displays real‑time conditions for each lab.
Lab tech checks dashboard at start of day.


Scenario 2 – Alarm Condition:

Temperature in Lab 3 > 28°C for >10 minutes.
Node continues to send readings; server detects pattern.
System generates an alert within 2 minutes.
Lab tech receives visual and/or email alert.
Tech inspects room, adjusts HVAC or notifies facilities.

Scenario 3 – Maintenance Mode:

Tech puts node into maintenance mode from dashboard.
Node stops generating alarms but still logs data.
Tech replaces sensor and runs a quick sanity check.




Explain how these scenarios would appear in the DCP‑100A ConOps section: - Give each scenario a name and brief description. - Use sequence lists or simple time‑sequence diagrams.
Emphasize: each scenario hints at later requirements, but we don’t yet formalize them as STR-* or REQ-*. That’s Stage 2–3.










{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  subgraph Lab["Teaching Lab"]
    S1["Sensor Node 1"]
    S2["Sensor Node 2"]
    S3["Sensor Node 3"]
  end

  GW["Wireless Gateway"]
  SRV["Campus Server<br>Data Storage & Processing"]
  UI["Web Dashboard<br>(Lab Technician)"]

  S1 --> GW
  S2 --> GW
  S3 --> GW
  GW --> SRV
  SRV --> UI







This is not architecture design yet; it’s a conceptual view of how things interact.
Helps you explain operational flow in ConOps: sensor → gateway → server → user.


Make the distinction clear: - This diagram at Stage 1 is descriptive: “There will likely be sensor nodes, some central gateway, some dashboard.” - Detailed protocols, exact hardware, and internal module breakdown belong in Stage 4.
But such a diagram helps stakeholders visualize the proposed solution class and operations.





Your Stage 1 work, step‑by‑step:

Clarify the project idea

From sponsor brief, faculty suggestion, or your team’s idea.
Rephrase it in your own words, check your understanding with advisor/sponsor.

Perform Business/Mission Analysis

Define the problem or mission clearly.
Analyze benefits, costs, and risks at a high level.

Identify Stakeholders

List stakeholders, their roles, and their interests.

Analyze Operational Context

Where and when will the system be used?
What external systems does it touch?

Develop ConOps (Scenarios & Modes)

Create several key operational scenarios that illustrate usage.

Check Feasibility & Scope

Ensure the project is doable within time and resources.

Write & Submit DCP‑100A

Integrate all the above into a coherent Project Proposal.



Encourage teams to iterate: - Talk to stakeholders → refine problem statement → update ConOps. - Don’t treat DCP‑100A as “one and done”; treat drafts as normal.
Advise them to start a shared document early and refine over 1–2 weeks rather than last‑minute.





A typical DCP‑100A might include these numbered sections:

Introduction & Background

Project title, team members, sponsor/advisor.
Brief background of application domain.

Problem / Mission Statement

Clear, concise summary of the problem or mission.

Business/Mission Objectives & Value

What improvements or outcomes are expected?

Stakeholder Identification & Roles

Table or diagram of stakeholders (role, interest, influence).

Operational Context & Constraints

Environment, existing systems, regulatory or safety constraints.

Concept of Operations (ConOps)

Operational scenarios
Modes of operation (normal, degraded, maintenance)

Preliminary Solution Class & Scope

Proposed type of solution (e.g., “wireless sensor network + web dashboard”).
Out‑of‑scope items, key assumptions.

Initial Risks and Feasibility Assessment

Major technical, schedule, or resource risks.



Mention: Your instructor may give a template for DCP‑100A – follow it, but this structure explains the intent behind each section.
Also note that DCP‑100A should be readable to a non‑technical stakeholder; avoid jargon when possible or explain it.







Problem / Mission Statement (example):

Teaching laboratories in the ECE department currently rely on manual checks by lab technicians to detect thermal issues that may damage equipment or create uncomfortable working environments. There is no central, real‑time view of lab conditions, leading to delayed detection of overheating and inefficient energy use.

Business/Mission Objectives:

Provide real‑time visibility of temperature/humidity in all teaching labs.
Enable early detection of overheating to protect equipment and occupants.
Support data‑driven energy optimization by facilities staff.


Stakeholder Snapshot (excerpt):








Stakeholder
Role
Interest / Concern




Lab Technicians
Primary users
Easy monitoring, reliable alerts


Facilities Manager
System owner/sponsor
Energy savings, reduced damage costs


IT Department
Network/security support
Secure, low‑maintenance integration


Faculty Instructors
Indirect users
Minimal disruption to teaching



ConOps Scenario (short excerpt):

Each morning, the lab technician opens a web dashboard that shows the current temperature and humidity in all labs. If any lab has been above 28°C for more than 10 minutes, the dashboard displays a red alert icon. The technician can click the alert to see a trend graph and notes, then decides whether to adjust HVAC or contact facilities.




Use this as a concrete illustration. Ask students whether this text answers: - What is the current pain? - What value does the new system provide? - Who are key stakeholders and what do they care about?
Highlight that no specific microcontroller or wireless protocol is mentioned yet – and that’s intentional.







Project concept: A wearable heart‑rate and activity monitor for post‑surgery patients, sending data to hospital staff.
Business/Mission Analysis (high‑level):

Mission: Reduce hospital readmissions by monitoring recovery at home.
Current state: Nurses rely on occasional phone calls and clinic visits.
Desired state: Continuous monitoring to detect issues early.


Stakeholders (initial list):

Patients (primary wearers)
Cardiologists and nurses (data users)
Hospital IT/security (data storage, privacy)
Regulatory bodies (medical device requirements)
Family members (secondary stakeholders)

ConOps scenario (simplified):

Before discharge, nurse configures wearable with patient ID and thresholds.
Patient wears device at home; it measures heart rate and activity continuously.
Device transmits periodic summaries and real‑time alerts via smartphone gateway.
Hospital dashboard flags patients whose metrics exceed pre‑set thresholds.
Nurse reviews alerts daily and follows up with selected patients.




Use this to show how Stage 1 for a medical device must already think about: - Regulatory aspects (data privacy, reliability). - Operational environment (home use, battery life, connectivity).
Again, emphasize: no need to specify exact ADC or BLE module in Stage 1. Focus is on context and operations.







Project concept: A scaled autonomous shuttle (small vehicle platform) for campus tours on predefined routes.
Business/Mission Analysis:

Mission: Demonstrate future autonomous transit, improve visitor experience.
Current state: Human‑driven carts for guided tours.
Desired outcomes: Reduced staffing, novel experience, data for research.


Stakeholders:

Campus visitors (passengers)
Shuttle operators / supervisors
Campus safety & risk management
Vehicle maintenance staff
Research group (using data/logs)

ConOps highlights:

Normal operation: shuttle follows route, stops at landmarks, announces info.
Abnormal: obstacle detected → shuttle slows/stops and requests human takeover.
Maintenance: daily pre‑drive check and periodic calibration by staff.




This example helps controls / robotics‑oriented students see that Stage 1 still applies: - Safety stakeholders and procedures must be recognized early. - Campus risk management might impose constraints (e.g., max speed, areas where driving is prohibited).
These factors must be visible in DCP‑100A, not discovered right before demo day.

You can annotate with keywords (e.g., “uses”, “maintains”, “funds”).

This helps reveal missing stakeholders: “Wait, who approves device installations in labs? Safety office?”





Quick exercise (5–7 minutes):

In groups (or individually), pick one of these example systems:

Campus bike‑sharing system with smart locks.
Remote lab access system for ECE experiments.
Smart parking lot occupancy system.

List as many stakeholder groups as you can think of.
Classify each as:

Primary user, indirect user, owner/sponsor, maintainer, regulator, course staff, etc.

Share 1–2 surprising stakeholders with the class.







Tip



When you pick a project idea for your team, do the same exercise and put the results into your DCP‑100A.



Actually run this if time allows. Use it to show them how quickly stakeholder lists expand beyond “the obvious one user.”
Capture common misses: - Security/IT. - Cleaning staff (for physical installations). - Other departments sharing the same lab space.





For the Stage 1 Gate Review (DCP‑100A), reviewers will look for:

Clear Problem / Mission Statement

Specific, understandable, not just “build X”.

Coherent Business/Mission Case

Why is this worth doing? Who benefits?

Comprehensive Stakeholder Identification

At least all major categories relevant to the project.

Concrete, Realistic ConOps

Several well‑thought‑out operational scenarios.

Realistic Scope & Feasibility

Fits within calendar, resources, and team skills.








Important



If your DCP‑100A cannot convince reviewers that you understand the problem, context, and stakeholders, you will likely not pass the Stage 1 Gate.



Be honest that failing Stage 1 gate is possible – but avoid scaring them unproductively. Frame it as: “We would rather catch unclear projects now than watch them collapse in Week 10.”
Encourage teams to ask for feedback on drafts early.





Common trap: > “We want to build a system using Raspberry Pi + LoRa + MQTT because it’s cool.”
Missing: Who needs what, and why?



What problem are we solving?
Who are the stakeholders, and what do they care about?
In what environment will this run (Wi‑Fi? cellular? no network)?
What constraints exist (power, safety, cost, privacy)?

Only then choose: microcontroller vs SBC vs FPGA, LoRa vs Wi‑Fi, etc.






Warning



“Technology shopping list” ≠ Stage 1. You must lead with needs, context, and ConOps.



You can tell a personal anecdote if you have one: a project that over‑specified its tech stack early and later discovered it didn’t fit stakeholder needs or constraints.
Reinforce: Stage 1 is designed to protect teams from premature tech commitment.

Note

Connect back to the earlier “Traceability: Backbone of the Project” slides from the course intro:

Stage 1 justifies why the later requirements exist.
During final reviews, we may ask: “Which mission objective does this requirement serve?”

That’s the upstream traceability from REQ-* and STR-* back to Stage 1.






Stage 1 – Project Framing & ConOps applies 29148’s:

Business or Mission Analysis
Stakeholder Identification

Your main job is to understand and document:

The problem/mission and business value.
The stakeholders and their roles.
The operational context and concept of operations.

The primary deliverable is DCP‑100A Project Proposal, which must:

Convince reviewers that the project is meaningful, feasible, and well‑framed.
Lay the foundation for Stage 2 stakeholder requirements (STR-*).

Strong Stage 1 work prevents late surprises and supports clean traceability through the V‑Model.







Important



Before you design circuits or write code, you must first engineer a well‑framed problem and mission. That is the essence of Stage 1.



Close by previewing what comes next:

Next session: we turn the stakeholder and mission understanding from Stage 1 into Stakeholder Requirements (Stage 2).
Suggest homework: draft a preliminary problem statement and identify at least 5 stakeholder groups for your project idea.

Invite specific questions about: - What level of detail is expected in DCP‑100A. - How to handle projects with unclear sponsors. - How to document uncertainty or assumptions in Stage 1.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Learning Objectives – Stage 1 Session
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain the purpose of Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations (ConOps).
Describe the 29148 Business or Mission Analysis process and its typical activities.
Describe the 29148 Stakeholder Identification process and why it is critical.
List the key contents and deliverables of DCP‑100A Project Proposal.
Apply these ideas to ECE capstone examples and avoid “solution first” thinking.


Emphasize: these are practical objectives. Students should walk away knowing exactly what they must produce for Stage 1 and why each part exists in industry practice.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
section: Where Stage 1 Fits in the V‑Model
text:
Where Stage 1 Fits in the V‑Model

















Note


Stage 1 lives at the very top‑left: - Define the business/mission need. - Understand the operational context. - Identify who cares (stakeholders).




Draw their attention to the highlighted Stage 1 box. Explain: if Stage 1 is weak or rushed, everything downstream (requirements, design, tests) will be misaligned.
Analogy: if you start building a bridge without agreeing on where it needs to connect and what it should carry (cars? trains?), the detailed design can be “beautiful” but completely wrong.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Stage 1 in the Course Framework


In course terms (from the intro deck):

V‑Model Level: Concept / Need
29148 Processes:

Business or Mission Analysis
Stakeholder Identification


Capstone Activities (Stage 1):

Define the problem domain and business/mission need.
Analyze existing solutions and their limitations.
Characterize environment & context:

Users
Constraints
External systems

Select a solution class (type of solution), not a design.
Produce a Concept of Operations (ConOps).


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑100A – Project Proposal

Problem statement
Stakeholder map
Operational scenarios
High‑level success criteria








Tip


Stage 1 is about the story of the project: Who has what problem, in what context, and what would “success” look like?





Connect back to the earlier “Course Stages Overview” slide. Tell them: DCP‑100A is the artifact that proves you did Stage 1 properly. Gate review for Stage 1 will focus strongly on: - Clarity of problem. - Completeness of stakeholder picture. - Realistic, well‑explained ConOps.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 – Processes in Stage 1


In Stage 1 we primarily apply:

Business or Mission Analysis

Why does this system exist?
What business or mission outcomes must it support?

Stakeholder Identification

Who cares about this system?
Who is affected, who operates it, who maintains it, who pays for it?


Later stages use other 29148 processes (Stakeholder Needs, System Requirements, etc.), but Stage 1 is the front door.














Clarify that 29148 is a requirements engineering standard. We’re using only a subset of its processes.
For this lecture, we’ll zoom into: - What Business/Mission Analysis looks like in a capstone context. - How to systematically identify stakeholders so nobody critical is forgotten.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
29148: Business or Mission Analysis – Concept
Purpose: To understand the business context or mission context that drives the need for a new or modified system.
Typical questions it answers:

What problem or opportunity are we addressing?
What is the mission (for non‑commercial systems) or business goal (for commercial ones)?
What outcomes do stakeholders care about?
What is the operational environment (physical, technical, organizational)?
What constraints limit possible solutions?







Important


If you can’t articulate the mission/business case, you don’t yet have a good project.




Use examples that students understand: - Mission: “Provide safe, reliable power to a remote village.” - Business goal: “Reduce lab equipment downtime by 30% to save maintenance costs.”
Explain: Engineers often get “assignments” like “build an app” or “design a sensor node,” but 29148 wants us to ask why first.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Business vs Mission – ECE Examples


Business‑oriented example:
Project: Smart Lab Energy Monitor

Business problem: Lab electricity bills are high; facilities wants to cut costs without compromising experiments.
Desired outcomes:

Identify energy‑hungry equipment and usage patterns.
Provide actionable data to lab managers.



Mission‑oriented example:
Project: Disaster‑Resilient Sensor Network

Mission context: Emergency services need situational awareness after earthquakes or floods.
Desired outcomes:

Maintain communication when mains power and some infrastructure are down.
Provide key sensor data (e.g., water level, gas leak detection) to responders.




Use these to highlight the difference: - Business: focus on cost, efficiency, competitive advantage. - Mission: focus on public safety, research goals, teaching, etc.
But both require clear problem framing and outcomes before we talk about specific hardware or software.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
29148: Business/Mission Analysis – Typical Activities
In 29148, activities commonly include:

Problem / Opportunity Definition

Describe current situation (AS‑IS).
Describe desired future situation (TO‑BE).

Context and Environment Analysis

Operational environment (physical, network, regulatory).
Systems and processes that interact with your proposed system.

Operations Concept Analysis

High‑level description of how the system will be used over time.

Feasibility Consideration

Technical feasibility (can we build it?).
Resource feasibility (time, budget, skills).

Business/Mission Case Formulation

Rationale for doing the project (benefits vs costs/risks).



Translate “standard‑speak” into capstone‑speak:

You’re not writing a full corporate business case, but you are expected to:

Clearly state the problem.
Analyze the context.
Outline how the proposed system will be used (ConOps).
Argue that the project is worth doing and feasible in two semesters.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Business/Mission Analysis vs Requirements












Stage 1:

What is the problem or mission?
In what context do we operate?
What high‑level outcomes do we want?

Stage 2 & 3:

Turn this context into requirements.
First in stakeholder language (Stage 2), then in technical form (Stage 3).








Note


Stage 1 sets the direction; Stages 2–3 formalize what exactly must the system do.




Sometimes students try to write detailed requirements in Stage 1. Clarify: Stage 1 should stop at “what problem, what context, what kind of solution”. Precision and testability come later.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
29148: Stakeholder Identification – Concept
Purpose: To identify all parties that have an interest in the system or are affected by it, so their needs can be captured later.
“Stakeholder” includes:

Direct end users
Operators and maintainers
Managers and decision‑makers
Safety or regulatory bodies
IT/network admins, lab technicians
Faculty advisors, sponsors, teaching staff (in our course)







Warning


Missing a critical stakeholder early → hidden requirements pop up late, causing rework, scope creep, or project failure.




Example: A team builds a lab monitoring system only thinking of professors as stakeholders. Later they discover: - Lab technicians need maintenance alerts. - IT department cares about network security. - Facilities team needs specific data formats.
Each “late discovery” means rework. Stakeholder identification aims to minimize that.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Stakeholder Types – ECE Capstone Context


Typical stakeholder roles in our projects:

Primary users

Students, lab technicians, clinicians, drivers, etc.

System owners / sponsors

Faculty PI, industry partner, lab manager.

Support and maintenance

IT support, lab techs, biomedical engineers.

Regulators / compliance

Safety office, ethics board, sometimes external standards (e.g., FCC, medical).



In this course specifically, also include:

Course staff

Instructor, TA → care about grading, documentation, safety.

University services

Campus network/security, facilities.

Future teams

Students who may extend or maintain your system next year.















Stress that “stakeholder” is broader than “the person who asked for the project.” Encourage teams to be inclusive when brainstorming stakeholders, then prioritize which ones are most critical for Stage 1.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Stakeholder Identification – Typical Activities
As described in 29148, tailored to our course:

Identify candidate stakeholders

Brainstorm a list of all who might interact with or be affected by the system.

Classify / group stakeholders

Users, owners, maintainers, regulators, external systems, etc.

Analyze stakeholder interests

What does each group want from the system?
What concerns or constraints do they have?

Prioritize stakeholders

Which ones are critical to success or acceptance?

Document a stakeholder map

For DCP‑100A: roles, interests, potential conflicts.








Tip


Treat stakeholder identification as a living activity – you may discover new stakeholders later, but Stage 1 should capture the obvious major ones.




Make this tangible: - Suggest using a simple table or diagram listing each stakeholder group and their key interests. - Mention that this feeds directly into Stage 2 (Stakeholder Requirements), where we turn these interests into STR-*.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
From 29148 Processes to Stage 1 Deliverables


29148 Says You Should:

Analyze business/mission context.
Identify and classify stakeholders.
Understand the operational environment.

In this course, you will:

Write a Problem Statement and Context section.
Create a Stakeholder Map.
Define Operational Scenarios / Use cases.
Propose a high‑level concept of operations (ConOps).


All of this goes into:
DCP‑100A – Project Proposal
Sections typically include:

Background & Problem Description
Business/Mission Need and Objectives
Stakeholder Identification & Roles
Operational Context & Environment
Concept of Operations (ConOps)
Preliminary Solution Class and Scope
Risks & Feasibility (high level)



Remind students: DCP‑100A does not lock every detail forever. But it does become a baseline for later traceability. If the mission or stakeholders change significantly later, that’s a controlled change, not a casual pivot.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
section: Concept of Operations (ConOps) – What It Is
text:
Concept of Operations (ConOps) – What It Is
A Concept of Operations (ConOps) is a narrative and graphical description of:

How the system will be used in its real environment.
Who interacts with it and what they do.
Sequences of events (before, during, after use).
Operational modes (normal operation, startup, shutdown, failure modes, maintenance).

It is written mainly in stakeholder language, not low‑level engineering details.






Note


Think of ConOps as a storyboard of your system in action.




Contrast ConOps with design docs: - ConOps: “Lab technician logs in, sees alerts, acknowledges them.” - Design: “We use MQTT broker X, JSON payload Y, database Z.”
In Stage 1 we focus on ConOps; specific design choices belong in later stages.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
ConOps – Typical Content (Adapted from 29148)
A good ConOps in DCP‑100A should cover:

Overview of the system’s purpose

One or two paragraphs in plain language.

Operational environment

Physical: lab, vehicle, hospital room, outdoor, etc.
Technical: network, power, existing systems.

Operational scenarios

Step‑by‑step story of typical use cases.
Include normal and abnormal (error/failure) cases.

Roles and responsibilities

Who does what during operation, setup, maintenance.

Assumptions and constraints

“We assume campus Wi‑Fi is available.”
“System must run on battery for at least 8 hours.”



Tell students: ConOps is where you clearly show reviewers that you understand real‑world use, not just technology.
Gate reviewers will ask: “Is this scenario realistic? Are key actors included? Are constraints recognized?”

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
section: Multi‑Scenario ConOps – Smart Lab Sensor Network
text:
Multi‑Scenario ConOps – Smart Lab Sensor Network


Example Project: Smart Lab Sensor Network (from intro deck).
Scenario 1 – Normal Operation:

Sensors in each lab periodically measure temperature and humidity.
Nodes send data wirelessly to a gateway.
Gateway forwards data to a campus server.
Dashboard displays real‑time conditions for each lab.
Lab tech checks dashboard at start of day.


Scenario 2 – Alarm Condition:

Temperature in Lab 3 > 28°C for >10 minutes.
Node continues to send readings; server detects pattern.
System generates an alert within 2 minutes.
Lab tech receives visual and/or email alert.
Tech inspects room, adjusts HVAC or notifies facilities.

Scenario 3 – Maintenance Mode:

Tech puts node into maintenance mode from dashboard.
Node stops generating alarms but still logs data.
Tech replaces sensor and runs a quick sanity check.



Explain how these scenarios would appear in the DCP‑100A ConOps section: - Give each scenario a name and brief description. - Use sequence lists or simple time‑sequence diagrams.
Emphasize: each scenario hints at later requirements, but we don’t yet formalize them as STR-* or REQ-*. That’s Stage 2–3.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Visualizing ConOps – High‑Level ECE Example












This is not architecture design yet; it’s a conceptual view of how things interact.
Helps you explain operational flow in ConOps: sensor → gateway → server → user.


Make the distinction clear: - This diagram at Stage 1 is descriptive: “There will likely be sensor nodes, some central gateway, some dashboard.” - Detailed protocols, exact hardware, and internal module breakdown belong in Stage 4.
But such a diagram helps stakeholders visualize the proposed solution class and operations.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Stage 1 Activities – Course‑Level View
Your Stage 1 work, step‑by‑step:

Clarify the project idea

From sponsor brief, faculty suggestion, or your team’s idea.
Rephrase it in your own words, check your understanding with advisor/sponsor.

Perform Business/Mission Analysis

Define the problem or mission clearly.
Analyze benefits, costs, and risks at a high level.

Identify Stakeholders

List stakeholders, their roles, and their interests.

Analyze Operational Context

Where and when will the system be used?
What external systems does it touch?

Develop ConOps (Scenarios & Modes)

Create several key operational scenarios that illustrate usage.

Check Feasibility & Scope

Ensure the project is doable within time and resources.

Write & Submit DCP‑100A

Integrate all the above into a coherent Project Proposal.



Encourage teams to iterate: - Talk to stakeholders → refine problem statement → update ConOps. - Don’t treat DCP‑100A as “one and done”; treat drafts as normal.
Advise them to start a shared document early and refine over 1–2 weeks rather than last‑minute.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
DCP‑100A – Suggested Structure
A typical DCP‑100A might include these numbered sections:

Introduction & Background

Project title, team members, sponsor/advisor.
Brief background of application domain.

Problem / Mission Statement

Clear, concise summary of the problem or mission.

Business/Mission Objectives & Value

What improvements or outcomes are expected?

Stakeholder Identification & Roles

Table or diagram of stakeholders (role, interest, influence).

Operational Context & Constraints

Environment, existing systems, regulatory or safety constraints.

Concept of Operations (ConOps)

Operational scenarios
Modes of operation (normal, degraded, maintenance)

Preliminary Solution Class & Scope

Proposed type of solution (e.g., “wireless sensor network + web dashboard”).
Out‑of‑scope items, key assumptions.

Initial Risks and Feasibility Assessment

Major technical, schedule, or resource risks.



Mention: Your instructor may give a template for DCP‑100A – follow it, but this structure explains the intent behind each section.
Also note that DCP‑100A should be readable to a non‑technical stakeholder; avoid jargon when possible or explain it.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
section: Example – Partial DCP‑100A for Smart Lab Sensor Network
text:
Example – Partial DCP‑100A for Smart Lab Sensor Network


Problem / Mission Statement (example):

Teaching laboratories in the ECE department currently rely on manual checks by lab technicians to detect thermal issues that may damage equipment or create uncomfortable working environments. There is no central, real‑time view of lab conditions, leading to delayed detection of overheating and inefficient energy use.

Business/Mission Objectives:

Provide real‑time visibility of temperature/humidity in all teaching labs.
Enable early detection of overheating to protect equipment and occupants.
Support data‑driven energy optimization by facilities staff.


Stakeholder Snapshot (excerpt):








Stakeholder
Role
Interest / Concern




Lab Technicians
Primary users
Easy monitoring, reliable alerts


Facilities Manager
System owner/sponsor
Energy savings, reduced damage costs


IT Department
Network/security support
Secure, low‑maintenance integration


Faculty Instructors
Indirect users
Minimal disruption to teaching



ConOps Scenario (short excerpt):

Each morning, the lab technician opens a web dashboard that shows the current temperature and humidity in all labs. If any lab has been above 28°C for more than 10 minutes, the dashboard displays a red alert icon. The technician can click the alert to see a trend graph and notes, then decides whether to adjust HVAC or contact facilities.



Use this as a concrete illustration. Ask students whether this text answers: - What is the current pain? - What value does the new system provide? - Who are key stakeholders and what do they care about?
Highlight that no specific microcontroller or wireless protocol is mentioned yet – and that’s intentional.

title: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
ECE Example – Embedded Medical Monitor


Project concept: A wearable heart‑rate and activity monitor for post‑surgery patients, sending data to hospital staff.
Business/Mission Analysis (high‑level):

Mission: Reduce hospital readmissions by monitoring recovery at home.
Current state: Nurses rely on occasional phone calls and clinic visits.
Desired state: Continuous monitoring to detect issues early.


Stakeholders (initial list):

Patients (primary wearers)
Cardiologists and nurses (data users)
Hospital IT/security (data storage, privacy)
Regulatory bodies (medical device requirements)
Family members (secondary stakeholders)

ConOps scenario (simplified):

Before discharge, nurse configures wearable with patient ID and thresholds.
Patient wears device at home; it measures heart rate and activity continuously.
Device transmits periodic summaries and real‑time alerts via smartphone gateway.
Hospital dashboard flags patients whose metrics exceed pre‑set thresholds.
Nurse reviews alerts daily and follows up with selected patients.



Use this to show how Stage 1 for a medical device must already think about: - Regulatory aspects (data privacy, reliability). - Operational environment (home use, battery life, connectivity).
Again, emphasize: no need to specify exact ADC or BLE module in Stage 1. Focus is on context and operations.

Strong Stage 1 work prevents late surprises and supports clean traceability through the V‑Model.







Important


Before you design circuits or write code, you must first engineer a well‑framed problem and mission. That is the essence of Stage 1.




Close by previewing what comes next:

Next session: we turn the stakeholder and mission understanding from Stage 1 into Stakeholder Requirements (Stage 2).
Suggest homework: draft a preliminary problem statement and identify at least 5 stakeholder groups for your project idea.

Invite specific questions about: - What level of detail is expected in DCP‑100A. - How to handle projects with unclear sponsors. - How to document uncertainty or assumptions in Stage 1.

title: Capstone Design
text:
From Stakeholder Needs to Engineer‑Ready Specifications
Capstone Design – Stage 3 Focus Session

This deck focuses on Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements in the course V‑Model framework.
Students should already have: - Completed or nearly completed Stage 2 / DCP‑100B with clear STR-* stakeholder requirements. - A good understanding of their stakeholders, needs, and operational scenarios from Stage 1–2.
Goals of this session: - Explain how to convert STR-* into System/Software Requirements (REQ-*). - Map this to ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 processes: - System / Software Requirements Definition - Requirements Analysis - Show how this becomes DCP‑200 – System Requirements Specification (SyRS/SRS). - Use ECE‑relevant examples (embedded, networks, signal processing, medical, etc.).
Emphasize: Stage 3 is about engineer‑friendly, testable, design‑independent requirements, not detailed architecture or implementation.

title: Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
text:
Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
From Stakeholder Needs to Engineer‑Ready Specifications
Capstone Design – Stage 3 Focus Session

This deck focuses on Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements in the course V‑Model framework.
Students should already have: - Completed or nearly completed Stage 2 / DCP‑100B with clear STR-* stakeholder requirements. - A good understanding of their stakeholders, needs, and operational scenarios from Stage 1–2.
Goals of this session: - Explain how to convert STR-* into System/Software Requirements (REQ-*). - Map this to ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 processes: - System / Software Requirements Definition - Requirements Analysis - Show how this becomes DCP‑200 – System Requirements Specification (SyRS/SRS). - Use ECE‑relevant examples (embedded, networks, signal processing, medical, etc.).
Emphasize: Stage 3 is about engineer‑friendly, testable, design‑independent requirements, not detailed architecture or implementation.

title: Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
text:
Learning Objectives – Stage 3 Session
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain the purpose of Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements in the V‑Model.
Describe the 29148 System/Software Requirements Definition process and its main activities.
Describe the 29148 Requirements Analysis process and what “good requirements” look like.
Write clear, testable System/Software Requirements (REQ-*) that are traceable from STR-*.
Relate these concepts to ECE capstone examples and to the DCP‑200 deliverable.


Tie back to Stage 2: “We captured what people want.” Stage 3: “Now we define what the system must do so engineers can design and test it.”

title: Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
section: Where Stage 3 Fits in the V‑Model
text:
Where Stage 3 Fits in the V‑Model

















Note


Stage 3 is the bridge between: - Stakeholder Requirements (STR-*) in Stage 2, and - Design & Implementation in Stages 4–5, and - Verification (VER-*) in Stages 6–7.




Explain the two “mirrors”: - Left side: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 (increasing technical detail). - Right side: 5 → 6 → 7 → 8 (increasing assurance).
Stage 3 is crucial because verification will measure the system against REQ-*.

title: Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
text:
Stage 2 → Stage 3 → Stage 4 – Big Picture












Stage 2: “Lab tech must be able to see all labs on one screen.” (STR-*)
Stage 3: “System shall support X labs, update every Y seconds, with Z error handling.” (REQ-*)
Stage 4: “We’ll implement this with microcontroller A, protocol B, backend C, etc.”







Tip


If Stage 3 is weak, design teams either: - Guess what to build, or - Design around assumptions instead of agreed requirements.




Highlight: Stage 3 is where you commit to the behaviors and qualities that you will later verify with tests.
Remind them: REQ-* can and should refer to measurable things: rates, errors, interfaces, formats, algorithms to some extent, etc.

title: Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
text:
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 – Relevant Processes for Stage 3
In Stage 3, we mainly use two 29148 processes:

System / Software Requirements Definition

Transform stakeholder requirements (STR-*) into system/software requirements (REQ-*).
Define what the system must do and how well, in technical but still design‑independent terms.

Requirements Analysis

Check requirements for quality: complete, consistent, unambiguous, feasible, verifiable, and traceable.
Resolve conflicts, partition requirements, and prepare for architecture and design.








Important


Stage 3 is not just “writing a list.” It is an engineering process: transform, refine, analyze, and organize requirements.




Make the mapping explicit: - STR-* → REQ-* is the “definition” step. - Checking, cleaning, grouping, and resolving issues is the “analysis” step.

title: Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
text:
29148: System / Software Requirements Definition – Purpose
Purpose (adapted from 29148): To establish a set of system and/or software requirements that correctly and completely:

Satisfy the stakeholder requirements (STR-*).
Define the system’s functions, performance, interfaces, constraints, and qualities.
Provide a basis for design and for verification of the system.

These requirements are typically documented in a System Requirements Specification (SyRS) or Software Requirements Specification (SRS).
For this course, we consolidate them into DCP‑200 – System Requirements.

Stress: REQ-* are written primarily for engineers and testers.
They should still be understandable, but they can use technical language (e.g., “HTTP over TLS”, “sampling frequency 1 kHz”) as long as they are traceable back to STR-*.

title: Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
text:
29148: System / SW Requirements Definition – Activities
Key activities you will perform in Stage 3:

Derive system/software requirements from stakeholder requirements

For each STR-*, ask: “What must the system actually do or guarantee?”
Create one or more REQ-* that satisfy that need.

Specify functional requirements

Define inputs, outputs, behaviors, modes, error handling.

Specify non‑functional requirements

Performance, reliability, security, safety, usability, etc., but now in technical, quantitative terms.

Define interfaces and external interactions

Hardware interfaces (sensors, actuators).
Communication interfaces (protocols, data formats, APIs).

Record assumptions and constraints

Platform assumptions, technology constraints, environmental bounds.


These outputs collectively form the System Requirements Specification (DCP‑200).

Make it concrete with an example:
STR-SY-001: “Lab techs see temp/humidity for each lab from one dashboard.”
Leads to REQ-* such as: - Number of labs the system must support. - Sampling rate and update intervals. - Data model (lab IDs, units). - Availability of an HTTP endpoint, etc.

title: Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
text:
29148: Requirements Analysis – Purpose
Purpose (adapted from 29148): To ensure that the set of requirements (REQ-*) is:

Correct – aligns with stakeholder intent.
Complete – covers necessary behaviors and qualities.
Consistent – no internal contradictions.
Unambiguous – each requirement has one clear meaning.
Feasible – realistic with technology, schedule, and budget.
Verifiable – testable by analysis, test, inspection, or demonstration.
Traceable – each REQ-* can be traced to at least one STR-* and later to design and test cases.







Note


Requirements Analysis is basically quality control for your requirements. You will iterate: write → check → refine → approve.




Encourage them to think of Requirements Analysis as a mini design review for requirements:

Review sessions within the team and with advisor.
Use checklists (we’ll give them) to catch common issues: ambiguous wording, mixing multiple requirements in one sentence, unrealistic performance numbers, etc.

title: Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
section: What Is a System / Software Requirement (REQ-*)?
text:
What Is a System / Software Requirement (REQ-*)?
A System or Software Requirement (REQ-*) is a statement that specifies:

A capability, function, performance level, interface, or constraint
That the system or software must satisfy,
Expressed in technical, design‑independent, testable terms.

Typical ID patterns in this course:

REQ-SY-### – overall system requirement.
REQ-SW-### – software‑specific requirement (e.g., backend, UI).
REQ-HW-### – hardware‑specific requirement (optional, if you partition).







Important


Key differences vs STR-*:

STR-*: stakeholder language, outcomes, scenarios.
REQ-*: technical language, specific behaviors and properties the system must show.





Use an example to highlight:

STR-SY-002: “System shall notify lab techs about overheating.”
REQ-SY-012: “When the measured temperature in any lab exceeds a configured threshold for more than a configured time, the system shall raise an alert event and send an email notification to all users with role ‘Lab Technician’ within 120 seconds.”

Stakeholder would sort of recognize their need, but the detail is for engineers/testers.

title: Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements
section: Example Chain: From STR-* to REQ-*
text:
Example Chain: From STR-* to REQ-*
Using the Smart Lab Sensor Network example:
Stakeholder Requirement:

STR-SY-001: Lab technicians shall be able to view the current temperature and humidity for each monitored lab from a single centralized dashboard.

Derived System Requirements (REQ-*):

REQ-SY-001: The system shall store, for each monitored lab, the latest measured temperature (°C) and relative humidity (%) values.
REQ-SY-002: The system shall provide a web‑based dashboard accessible over HTTP(S) that displays the current temperature and humidity for all configured labs on a single page.
REQ-SY-003: The system shall refresh displayed sensor values at least once every 60 seconds during normal operation.

Each REQ-* is:
